coredata.dc:description: A green inhibitor of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was produced by combining succinic acid with hexamethylenediamine (N-HCDs) or ethylenediamine (N-ECDs). Several specialized tools were then used to assess the structure and corrosion-prevention capabilities of the HCl solution. The inhibitory mechanism of N-ECDs and N-HCDs was analyzed using quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics. Electrochemical data indicated that both acted as mixed-type adsorbent inhibitors. N-ECDs and N-HCDs effectively prevented carbon steel corrosion, with maximum inhibition efficiencies of 94.5 % and 93.6 % at 80 ppm, respectively. The absorption of N-ECDs and N-HCDs involved both chemisorption and physisorption, following the Langmuir adsorption model. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirmed the formation of a protective layer of inhibitor molecules on the carbon steel surface. Additionally, molecular dynamics simulations combined with Density Functional Theory (DFT) parameters provided theoretical insights into the adsorption processes occurring at specific active sites.

head.ce:title: Efficient enhancement of corrosion resistance of steel in HCl environment using N-doped succinic acid-based carbon quantum dots: A comprehensive quantum and electrochemical study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green inhibitor of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was produced by combining succinic acid with hexamethylenediamine (N-HCDs) or ethylenediamine (N-ECDs). Several specialized tools were then used to assess the structure and corrosion-prevention capabilities of the HCl solution. The inhibitory mechanism of N-ECDs and N-HCDs was analyzed using quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics. Electrochemical data indicated that both acted as mixed-type adsorbent inhibitors. N-ECDs and N-HCDs effectively prevented carbon steel corrosion, with maximum inhibition efficiencies of 94.5 % and 93.6 % at 80 ppm, respectively. The absorption of N-ECDs and N-HCDs involved both chemisorption and physisorption, following the Langmuir adsorption model. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirmed the formation of a protective layer of inhibitor molecules on the carbon steel surface. Additionally, molecular dynamics simulations combined with Density Functional Theory (DFT) parameters provided theoretical insights into the adsorption processes occurring at specific active sites.

ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have widely utilized fluorescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots [1], carbon dots [2], polymer dots [3], fluorescent nanodiamonds [4], and fluorescent nanoclusters [5], because of theirsignificant uses in photoelectricdevices, biomedicine, etc. Specifically, carbon-based nanomaterials are garnering more and more interest because of their clear advantages in terms of stability, environmental friendliness, etc. Since its first publication in 2004, carbon dots (CDs) a novel fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial have garnered much interest due to their exceptional optical qualities such as strong absorption, brilliant photoluminescence, outstanding light stability, and resistance to light bleaching, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, good biocompatibility, ease of preparation, and superior light stability [6–9]. A considerable multitude of carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized and exhibited remarkable advantages across diverse application domains., including biomedicine [10], photovoltaic devices [11], anticounterfeiting [12], sensing [13], catalysis [14], light-emitting diode (LED) [15] and so on.

Following prolonged operation, temperature exchange systems and cooling elements within an industrial coolant loop are prone to accumulation and corrosion, leading to the formation of encrustations on the inner surfaces of the equipment machinery [16–18]. The process of pickling stands as the predominant and highly effective method for the removal of such contaminants. In industrial settings, inorganic acids including HCl acid, H2SO4 acid, H3PO4 acid, and HF acid are commonly employed for pickling purposes. Amidst these, hydrochloric acid and sulfuric acid emerge as the mostconsistently utilized options.Nevertheless, these acids may cause substantial corrosion to metalsand alloys [19,20]. Therefore, inorganic or organic based corrosion inhibitors are carefully selected to mitigate or prevent the corrosive effects induced by these acids during the process of pickling, corrosion buildupsremoval processes applied to steel surfaces [21–25]. While these corrosion inhibitors work well to prevent metals or alloys from corroding, they also include certain dangerous elements that might endanger human health or cause significant environmental contamination. Safeguarding the environment has become increasingly important to individuals, particularly over the last few years, and pickling corrosion inhibitor research and use must meet strict guidelines [26–28]. The following research phase will progressively shift toward developingconscious pickling compounds with great corrosion mitigation efficacy, reduced pollution and environmental impact, and affordable [29,30].

In conjunction with the corrosion of metallic surfaces, CDs may serve as anticorrosion nanofillers to improve the corrosion mitigation performance of certain polymers, by leveraging the interactionsat the interface between adjacent CDs or between polymer chains and CDs, along with the enhanced oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) outputs for CDs [31,32]. Meanwhile,on the basis of the molecular composition of classical corrosion inhibitors, there is theoretical potential forheteroatom-doped CDs, as eco-friendly inhibitors, to mitigate metal corroding viaphysical electrostatic interactions or the creation of coordinated linksamong the metal atoms and heteroatoms. To date, heteroatom-doped CDs feature successful bottom-up preparation (for instance solvothermal, hydrothermal, and a combination of microwave-assisted hydrothermal/solvothermal methods)involving the inclusion of smaller heteroatom-comprising molecules into the synthesis systems, wherein nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) gained considerable appeal owed to amplenitrogen-comprising precursors and their size correspondence with C atoms [33,34]. As mentioned earlier, N doping induces the creation of a structure labeled as N-type (pyrrolic N and pyridinic) within CDs [35,36]. In this structure, pyrrolic N has the ability to form coordination bonds with metals, in order to improve CDs adsorption. Following this, substantial quantities of N-CDs produced with a variety of nitrogenous precursors and synthesizing techniques were employed in succession as efficient corrosion inhibitors for metals in different aggressivemedia [37–42].

In this paper, we have prepared N-ECDs and N-HCDs with Succinic acid as carbon source, and ethylenediamine andhexamethylenediamine as dopants, subsequently the easy and environmentally friendly hydrothermal treatment with hexamethylenediamine and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The N-ECDs and N-HCDs are characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy,ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, andX-ray diffraction (XRD). The corrosion mitigation of the N-ECDs and N-HCDs for carbon steel in the acidicmedium is investigated using electrochemical techniques and surface analysis methods.The inhibition mechanisms of the N-ECDs and N-HCDsin 1 M HCl solution are discussed and described in detail.Furthermore,tocorroborate the experimental results, the interaction between the inhibitor and carbon steel was investigated more thoroughly utilizing quantum computational techniques including Molecular Dynamics Simulation (MDS) and Density Functional Theory (DFT).

Succinic acid, hexamethylenediamine, and ethylenediamine were provided by Sigma Aldrich. The carbon steel was chosen for the testing electrode with the composition Fe: base; C: 0.350–0.390; Mn: 0.50–0.80; Si: ≤0.400; S: 0.015–0.035; and Cr + Ni + Mo: ≤0.63, which was cleaned by ultrasonic apparatus in absolute ethyl alcohol and underwent polishing by sandpapers of 200, 600, 800 and 1200 grid.

The steps involved in synthesizing N-HCDs and N-ECDs are shown in Fig. 1 . Ten milliliters of water were sonicated to dissolve an equimolar combination of succinic acid (0.198 g, 3 mmol) and hexamethylenediamine (0.18 g, 3 mmol) or ethylenediamine (0.18 g, 3 mmol). The dissolved mixture was then put in an autoclave and heated for eighteen hours at 200 °C. Subsequently, the crude product underwent a one-day purification process using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off ∼3.0 kDa) to eliminate nonreactive molecules. Upon solvent extraction and further vacuum drying, N-ECDs and N-HCDs were produced.

The studied medium consisted of 1 M HCl solution includingvarious concentrations of N-doped CDs(0, 20, 40, 60, 80 mg/L). Prior to preparation, the test solution underwent a dilution from a concentrated hydrochloric acid (37 %).

The produced N-doped CDs were characterized employing a SHIMADZU IRAffinity-1S spectrometer at room temperature and were analyzed using FTIR. The spectra acquired covered the range of 4000 to 500 cm−1. A UVmini-1240/UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis spectrum. The N-doped CDs were examined using the Mira 3 SEM model. The thermal investigation was conducted using SHIMADZU DTG-60H, while XRD measurements were carried out using BRUKER D8 ADVANCE equipped with a Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation.

A 1 M HCl solution containing various concentrations of N-doped CDs was used as the corrosive medium for the electrochemical test, which was collected using a PGZ301 electrochemical workstation at room temperature. The experimental prototype was a standard three-electrode system with a reference electrode that was a saturated calomel electrode, a counter electrode made of a platinum plate, and a working electrode made of carbon steel. The open circuit potential (OCP) must reach a stable condition for thirty minutes prior to testing. Tafel curves were acquired by monitoring the potential at a rate of 0.5 mV/s in the potential area of ±250 mV around the OCP. Every sample in the EIS test was measured at 105 – 0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV.

Following the electrochemical test, distilled water was used to clean the carbon steel, A scanning electron microscope (SEM, S4800, Japan) was used to examine the specimen's corrosion shapeof dried sample.

Gauss View 6.1 program wasused to build up and visualize the input an output files, as well as to plot and extract all necessary information. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian09 series of programs. Geometries of the investigatedspecies, includingethylenediamine (EDA), hexamethylenediamine (HMDA), succinicacid (SA), hexanoicacid (HA), and octanoicacid (OA), wereoptimizedusingDensity functional theory (DFT) by employing B3LYP functional [43]in conjunctionwith 6–311 + G(d,p) basis set. The B3LYP functionshown to produce consistent geometries for a wide range of systems [44]. Optimization processes wereperformed in an aqueous solution usingthe polarized continuum model of solvation (PCM) [45]. Frequency calculation at the same level of theoryfollowed the optimization process to ensure that all structures are minima to the potentialenergy surfacewith no imaginaryfrequencies. Single point calculationsusing the natural bond orbital analysis code (NBO) [46] werealsoperformed to compute the natural population analysis (NPA) and the Hirshfeld charges for the systems of N, N + 1, and N-1 electronsusing the optimizedgeometries of the neutral species.

The adsorption energywascalculatedusingPerdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE)/projector-augmented wave (PAW) based DFT as implemented in Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP). With an energycut-off set at 400 eV. For geometryoptimization,

self-consistent iterationswereapplied. Based on the findings of previousstudies [53], Fe (110) has the densest structure and the lowestenergyamong Fe surfaces. In thisstudy, the Fe (110) surface iscomposed of 4 layers and 4x5 supercells. Monhkorst-Pack 4 × 4 × 1 [54] and Fermi-levelsmearing of 0.1 eV wasused. Grimme's DFT-D3 approach [55]to the treatment of van der Waals (VDW) forces wasapplied. The vacuum layer was set with 30 Å in the Z direction to prevent interlayer interference. The Poisson-Boltzmann implicitsolvation model in VASP-sol [56] was used to simulate the solvationeffects in an aqueous solution (waterdielectric constant equal to 80). ART-FTIR was implemented to characterize the N-ECDs and N-HCDs specimens. Distinct wave numbers for the absorption peaks indicate distinct functional group characteristics. The significant absorption maxima at 3310 cm−1 in the powder sample of N-ECDs and N-HCDs are caused by the N–H and O–H, as shown in Fig. 2
a [57]. The C–H bond is connected to the weak peak at 2954 cm−1 [58]. The characteristic double link absorption wavelengths for C=O are the faint peaks at 1672 cm−1 [59]. Stretching vibrations of C-N are attributed to the peak found at 1450 cm−1 [60]. Peak at 1265 cm−1 attributed to thestretching vibrations of C–O [61]. Furthermore, establishing the N–C=O bond at 1510 cm−1 [62],suggested that the hexamethylenediamine, ethylenediamine, and succinic acid had a good reaction.

Two distinctive absorption bands can be seen in the UV–vis pattern of N-ECDs and N-HCDs, as seen in Fig. 2 b. In organic substances, the π–π* and n–π* transitions are often associated with the first absorption band at 253 nm and the second band at 297 nm, respectively [63]. These transitions show the presence of double-bond and lone-pair electrons in functional groups in the specimen. Electrons cause them in the π orbitals, moving to the π* orbitals or from the non-valence n orbitals to the π* orbitals. When integrating the data from the FTIR, the C=O bond's π–π* transition will probably take place at 253 nm, and the n–π* transition at 297 nm will be associated with either the N–H or O–H electronic transitions [64].

X-ray diffraction/scattering is the only approach used in this investigation that only offers information on the CD's core. Fig. 2 shows the XRD pattern of N-ECDs and N-HCDs. N-HCDs have a far higher level of crystallinity than N-ECDs according to the intensity and sharpness of the peaks. Two faint peaks at 2θ = 20° and 2θ = 22° in the XRD pattern of N-ECDs compared to N-HCDs show a reduced crystalline size, which may be related to disruptions of the non-repeatable side of ethylenediamine in the structure of N-ECDs. However, four peaks are detected in the XRD patterns for N-HCDs that are finer than those for N-ECDs: 2θ = 19°, 2θ = 22°, 2θ = 25°, and 2θ = 27° [65]. The hexamethylenediamine's greater homogeneity and repeatability make the crystallite develop, making it larger than an N-ECD. On the contrary hand, lower crystal plane distances manifested in larger 2θaccording to the brag equation. The peaks associated with N-HCDs are situated to the right of those associated with N-ECDs as shown in Fig. 2 c. This might be due to the additional tight structure observed in N-HCDs [66].

Fig. 3 shows the time-dependent OCP evolution of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor and with CDs inhibitor sample. As immersion duration increased, all OCP curves stabilized. In general, larger OCP values indicate decreased corrosion susceptibility for metal substrates. In comparison to the blank HCl solution, the stable OCP values of inhibitor-containing samples increased, As the concentration of CDs inhibitor sample in the electrolyte evolved, the OCP value rose to more noble levels. The higher positive potential resulted in a decreased corrosion rate. These findings suggest that the N-ECDs and N-HCDs corrosion inhibitors may provide excellent anticorrosion performance in severe environments.

The polarization curve results are shown in Fig. 4 . Meanwhile, Table 1 displays the Tafel characteristics for the CDs inhibitor sample obtained using the Tafel extrapolation technique. These parameters included corrosion current (icorr), the potential of corrosion (Ecorr), both anodic and cathodic Tafel slope (βaandβc), the degree of surface coverage (θ), and lastly the inhibition efficacy (η). The following equations are utilized for calculating θ and I.EPDP [67]:

By introducing both N-ECDs and N-HCDs, the Ecorr drastically fell and shifted positively, indicating that the inhibitor might inhibit the anode reaction. Simultaneously, the icorr value declined with a rise in N-ECD and N-HCD content, indicating a reduction in the degree of corrosion. Furthermore, once the inclusion of inhibitors, the values of βa and βc shift, suggesting a change in the anodic and cathodic current density, supporting the mixed type classification of N-ECDs, and N-HCDs [68]. It is possible to show that the looked-into inhibitor behaves as an assortment of form reactions primarily anodic type reaction, by comparing the measured corrosion potential values after adding N-ECDs and N-HCDs to the values before introducing the inhibitor [69].It is significant to note that when N-ECDs and N-HCDs concentration rises, icorr values noticeably decline demonstrating how effectively N-ECDs and N-HCDs lower the corrosion rate of carbon steel. At a concentration of 80 ppm, the I.EPDP values of N-ECDs and N-HCDs ranged from 85 to 94.5 % and 79.8 % to 94.4 %, respectively. It is, therefore, possible to conclude that a stable layer of N-ECDs and N-HCDs adsorbed onthe surface of carbon steel forms, acting as a barrier toward both the anodic and cathodic current density values as well as metal dissolution at the anodic and cathodic sites in 1 M HCl solution [70,71].

The EIS method investigated the process-related resistance developed at the contact point between the carbon steel and the solution. This approach yields trustworthy findings because the metal is not significantly polarized and does not disrupt the metal/solution interaction. Additionally, the appearance of the curves collected provides valuable information on the processes behind the reactions occurring on the surface. Fig. 5 displays the results collected in 1 M HCl solution in inhibited and uninhibited solutions with varying N-ECD and N-HCD concentrations. ZView software was used to fit the experimental findings to the EIS data, and the results are shown in Table 2 . In order to achieve this goal, the carbon steel/solution interface was defined by the electrical equivalent circuit diagram (EECD) seen in Fig. 5. This EECD is made up of the constant phase element (CPE), polarization resistance (Rp), and solution resistance (Rs). Equation (3) was used to determine the percentage of the corrosion mitigation activity (I.EEIS %) of the N-ECDs and N-HCDs for carbon steel exposed to 1 M HCl solution [72].

and RPstands for the resistance of polarization obtained for CS in the absence and presence of N-ECDs and N-HCDs, respectively.

Upon examining the carbon steel Nyquist plot produced in the blank HCl solution, one observes a depressed capacitive semi-circle. The associated Bode charts allowed for the detection of the parallel observations. Similar findings regarding steel corrosion in HCl medium have been documented in the literature [73–76]. The charge transfer resistance (Rct), the diffusion resistance (Rd), and the accumulation resistance at the interface steel/solution (Ra), also responsible for creating a barrier effect, are all included within the single semi-circle. The total resistance is referred to as polarization resistance, or Rp (Rp = Rct + Rd + Ra) [77]. The capacitive loop demonstrates that, in the molar HCl solution, the carbon steel corrosion process occurs under activation control [78]. A capacitive loop is a complete semi-circle in theory. Surface inhomogeneity and frequency dispersion are often blamed for the capacitive loop's departure from ideality [79,80].

The curves produced for N-ECDs and N-HCDs were influenced by concentration. When 20 ppm of N-ECDs and N-HCDs were incorporated into the corrosive medium. However, compared to the blank solution, the capacitive loop's width, the associated resistance Rp, and I.EEIS% (Table 2) all improved. These findings suggest that, even at low concentrations, N-ECDs and N-HCDs block the corrosion response without altering the mechanism. The I.EEIS% was found to rise considerably at concentrations of 80 ppm, indicating that thicker films with higher quality more effectively develop on the metal surface. This discovery suggests that N-ECDs and N-HCDs impede the corrosive assault on the metal surface by acting via adsorption on the steel surface [81].

The incorporation of N-ECDs and N-HCDs causes the subsidence of n values, as Table 2 illustrates, suggesting that their non-uniform adsorption on CS. The fact that the n value is less than 1 (in the range of 0.82–0.89) indicates that the metal/solution contact does not operate as an ideal capacitor [82,83].

Only a one-time constant is seen in the impedance spectra shown in Bode plots (Fig. 5), suggesting that the electron transfer process occurs at the metal/electrolyte interface. Furthermore, the phase angle shift changes as the concentration of the studied carbon dots increases, which is directly linked to N-ECDs and N-HCDs adsorption into the CS surface and the subsequent development of a protective layer [84].

The kinetics of metal corrosion are greatly influenced by temperature, which also significantly affects how inhibitors adsorb. Weak physical interactions that cause inhibitor adsorption, such as electrostatic attraction, diminish as temperature rises. On the other hand, when the inhibitor's efficacy stays the same or marginally rises with temperature, the results suggest that the inhibitory compound is likely chemisorbed and creates a solid chemical connection with the metallic substrate [85,86]. It is important to remember that corrosion inhibitors often behave this way in acidic solutions and may behave differently in response to changes in experimental settings. In this paper, the effect of temperature (from 293 to 323 K) on the mitigation of corrosion by N-ECDs and N-HCDs at optimal concentrations was studied as well for carbon steel. The investigation was centered on several corrosion metrics, including I.EPDP, icorr, and Ecorr. We examined how temperature affected the Tafel curves for both inhibitor-free medium and ones that contained N-ECDs and N-HCDs. The acquired findings are illustrated in Fig. 6 and Table 3 .

A significant rise in corrosion current density values of carbon steel submerged in HCl solution may be attributed to rapid deterioration at higher temperatures, as shown by the results in Fig. 6 and Table 3. The inhibitory action of N-ECDs and N-HCDs is responsible for the considerable reduction in the degradation impact seen when the inhibitor is introduced to the HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition efficiencies values marginally rise with temperature, suggesting that N-ECDs and N-HCDs adsorb thoroughly, with chemisorption predominating, on the surface of carbon steel. The acquired data show that adding corrosion inhibitors considerably shifts the Tafel plots in all systems. Since increasing temperatures activate the hydrogen and chloride ions, temperature impacts cause this change. Corrosion activity rises as a result of this activation [87]. Researchers commonly use the following expression to try to determine the activation energy of the corrosion process [88]:
(15)
i
corr
= A exp
(
-
E a
RT
)
Where A stands for the Arrhenius constant, T for the absolute temperature, R for the gas constant, and icorr for the current corrosion density. Ea is the activation energy of the corrosion process. The Arrhenius curve of (ln icorr) vs (1/T) for carbon steel in 1MHCl media is shown in Fig. 7
a. The graph's slope may be used to determine the activation energy of the corrosion process. Eq.(5) was applied to compute the standard enthalpy (ΔHa) and entropy (ΔSa) of activation [89]:
(16)
i
corr
=
RT
Nh

(
Δ
S a

)

(

)
Where N is Avogadro's number, h stands for Planck's constant, and icorr is the current corrosion density. Plotting ln icorr/T against 1/T for the dissolving of carbon steel in 1MHCl in the presence and absence of various amounts of artificial inhibitors allowed for the determination of the thermodynamic parameters. Fig. 7 bshowed the dashed lines that were produced. From Table 4
the dissolving process of carbon steel is endothermic, as shown by the positive sign of the enthalpy [90]. The development of an orderly, stable adsorption film on the steel surface is indicated by the ΔSa value, which displays a negative increment by incorporating 80 ppm N-ECDs and N-HCDs (−221.71 J. mol−1. K−1 and −220.87 J. mol−1. K−1) when compared to the blank solution (−117.50 J. mol−1. K−1) [91]. The un-inhibited system's activation energy value in 1 M HCl is 40.63 KJ.mol−1, whereas N-ECDs and N-HCDs have 16.86 and 16.53 KJ.mol−1 values, respectively. It is possible to estimate how temperature will affect the inhibitory performance by comparing the activation energies for the blank and the inhibitor-containing conditions. Radovici's categorization [92] leads one to the conclusion that, in this instance, the inhibitor adsorbs onto the steel surface via chemical adsorption.

Stated differently, a rise in temperature causes the inhibition process to occur faster. As a result, the inhibited solution's Ea is lower than the uninhibited solution's. N-ECDs and N-HCDs contain a large amount of oxygen and nitrogen heteroatoms, which, at high temperatures, exhibit chemisorption to establish a solid coordinating connection with metal. The lower values of Ea values in the presence of both N-ECDs and N-HCDs, if compared to the blank test solution, corroborate the statements made in this study.

Several adsorption isotherm models may be used to understand the relationships between the metal's surface and the corrosion-inhibitory chemicals. Instead of often approaching equilibrium, the adsorption of organic compounds on a metallic surface usually stabilizes into a chronic adsorption state. When corrosion inhibitory chemicals are present, the corrosion rate reduces, and the adsorption mechanism evolves to approach a near-steady state. In general, two interactions comprising physical or chemical adsorption define this adsorption. The way that organic products (inhibitors) adsorb affects considerably how effective they are in stopping corrosion. The interaction may also be studied using several adsorption isotherm models [93–95], such as Freundlich, Temkin, Frumkin, and Langmuir, all of which have been well covered in the literature. They were applied to values of surface coverage (θ) with different doses of inhibitors [96,97]. By linearly fitting several adsorption isotherms, θ data were visually verified for an array of N-ECDs and N-HCDs doses (Table 1). For both carbon dots compounds, the most accurate depiction of the adsorption was found by the recovery of the Langmuir adsorption. The following equation describes the Langmuir isotherm [98]:
(17)
C / θ = 1 /
K
ads
+ C
where C designatesthe concentration of N-ECDs or N-HCDs, and Kads defines the adsorptionequilibrium constant. The representation of (C/θ) as a function ofθ exhibited lines (Fig. 8 ) having correlation coefficient (R2) valuesof 0.9999 and 0.9996 for N-ECDs and N-HCDs, respectively.

The derived descriptors are exploited to assessthestandard Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔG° ads), by applying Eq. (7) [99]:
(18)
Δ
G 0
a d s = - R T L n
( C H 2 O K a d s )
In this formula, T designates the temperature (K) and R refers to the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol.K). H2O concentration in the studied solution was claimed as 1000 g/L. Both N-ECDs and N-HCDs exhibit R2 values nearly unitary, insinuating the adsorption of N-ECDs and N-HCDs on the steel surface corresponds to the Langmuir isotherm. Using (7), Kads values were used to get the conventional Gibbs adsorption-free energy. Based on these data, carbon steel's exceptionally stable surface spontaneous adsorption process is shown by the negative sign of ΔG° ads. Additionally, it has been reported that the absolute values of ΔG° adsare approximately −20 kJ/mol or less, which indicates interactions between charged compounds and charged metals. This indicates physical adsorption, whereas −40 KJ/mol or more negative values indicate chemical adsorption of the inhibitor with the metal surface [100]. As a result, the computed values of ΔG° ads in this study ranged from −20 KJ/mol to −40 KJ/ mol. It may be claimed that the two inhibitory chemicals on the steel's surface follow both physisorption and chemisorption mechanisms.

The steel samples were submerged in HCl solution containing N-ECDs and N-HCDs at room temperature for twenty-four hours. Fig. 9
displayed the surface morphology as determined by SEM. After being submerged in HCl solution without a corrosion inhibitor for 24 h, the steel's surface was severely damaged, and many pits and corrosion products emerged. It was evident that steel corrosion was improved using a few as-prepared inhibitors. The N-ECDs and N-HCDs inhibitors were shown to have a protective effect on the electrode in an acidic media, and the steel surface was smooth and well-guarded. This might be connected to the larger surface area the high inhibitor concentration covers. As a consequence, there was an improvement in the steel's surface covering and excellent protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HOMOs, LUMOs, and ESP maps
ce:section.ce:para: The optimizes structures, HOMOs, LUMOs, electrostatic potential maps (ESP), total densities at HOMO (TD-HOMO) and at LUMO (TD-LUMO) maps of EDA, HMDA, SA, HA, and OC as obtainedusing B3LYP/6–311 + G(d,p) level of theory are shown in Fig. 10 .

A close viewing of Fig. 10 shows that the HOMOs and LUMOs of EDA, HMDA, and SA are located over the entiremoiety of the molecules. The HOMOs are distributed over the more electronegativeatoms (N and O), indicatingtheirability to donateelectronsduring the chemicalreactions to form HA and OA,while the LUMOs are redistributed at the adjacent C atoms, showing their ability to accept electrons during the reactions to produce HA and OA. The HOMO of HA is located on the amino-oxobutanoicacid, reflecting the capability of the nitrogen-amino group and oxygencarbonyl group to donateelectrons to the metal surface. Whereas, the LUMO ismainlydistributed on the ethylene group adjacent to the amine group, reflecting the ability of C atoms to acceptelectronsfrom the metal surface. Similarly, the HOMO of OA is distributed over the N atom of one of the amine groups, while the LUMO isredistributed over the middle part of the moleculethatcontains the n-butyl moiety. The electron-rich (nucleophilic centers) and electron-poor (electrophilic centers) parts of the molecules are represented by strongred and strong blue colors, respectively. Thesemaps, as well as the TD-HOMO and TD-LUMO maps, confirmed the results of HOMOs and LUMOs surfaces. For HA and OA molecules, the strongredcolorcovers the N and O atoms, whilethe strongbluecolorcovers the adjacent C atoms, reflectingthat the formers can act as electrophilicattack centers, while the latter can act as nucleophilicattack centers.

Inspection of Table5 indicates that both inhibitors have almost the sameenergy gap (N-HCDs = 6.304 eV) and N-ECDs = 6.356 eV), reflecting that both inhibitors have almost the chemical reactivity, and hence, their inhibition efficiencies are almost similar, which agree with the experimental study.

The other global descriptors als oconfirm the results of the energy gaps. It is found that the electronegativity, hardness and softness, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, electron-donating (accepting) power and net electrophilicity of both compounds are almost similar, with differences of an average of 0.004 eV, reflecting that both compounds have almost the same chemical reactivity, and consequently they have the same ability to protect the metal against corrosion. The positive values of the fraction of electron transfer (N-ECDs (0.171) and N-HCDs (0.173) indicate that both inhibitors have the ability to donate electrons to the unoccupied 3d-orbitals of the metal during the protection process. Finally, both inhibitors are characterized by higher dipole moments (N-ECDs (8.3 Debye) and N-HCDs (9.0 Debye), which are higher than that of water (1.8 Debye), illustrating the capability of both inhibitors to expel water from the surface of the metal and can act as good anticorrosive materials.

The effects of structural difference on the reactivity of the different active centers in the molecule were explained by Kenichi Fukui [103]in terms of the Fukui function (FFs), which explains the behavior of electrons in a molecule when exposed to small perturbation [104]. FFs can beused to identify specific regions or atoms within a molecule that can serve as nucleophilic,electrophilic, or free radical centers [105]. 3D iso-surfaces of the Fukui function for the most active electrophilic (

Table SI1 of the supplementarymaterialsgathered all the results for the non-hydrogenatoms. In addition, Fig. SI1 of the supplementary materials shows the optimized structures of the probed inhibitors with the number of atoms. Our results in Table 6 show that the most active centers for electrophilic in both inhibitors correspond to the nitrogen atoms, which carry the lone-pair electrons, while the most active centers for nucleophilicattacks are the carbonatomsthat are bonded to the nitrogenatoms. For N-ECDs,the centers N6 and N13, O9, and N9 correspond to the mostfavoredcenters for electrophilic attacks, while the O24, O23, and C22 centers are the mostfavored centers for nucleophilic attacks. ForN-HCDs, the N28 and N23 centers stand for electrophilic attacks, while the C6, C7, and C5 belong to the mostfavored centers for nucleophilic attacks.

An illustration of inhibitors' adsorption on the Fe (110) surface isshown in Fig. 13 along with their adsorption energies. Inhibitors containing carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups (succinicacid, octanoicacid, and hexanoicacid) are all chemically bonded to surfaces through interaction between Fe and O atoms. In addition, hexamethylenediamine and ethylenediamineinhibitors are also chemically bonded to the surface by the covalent dative bond (N-Fe). The adsorption energies (
E
a
d
s
)
of the five inhibitors on the Fe surface are negative and ranged between −202–−102 kJ/mol. That indicates the process is spontaneous and stable. The higher
E
a
d
s
values are indicative of the chemisorption process and expected that these inhibitors have strong inhibition efficiencies.

Interestingly, it was found that hexamethylenediamine@Feis the most stable among the other investigated inhibitors with
E

of −202.34 kJ/mol. Due to form two types of bonds between hexamethylenediamineand Fe surface: i) covalent dative bond between

, and ii) multi non-classical hydrogen bonds

Followed by N-ECDs@Fethenethylenediamine@Fe. On the other hand, SA@Fe and N-HCDs@Fe are the last stable with
E

of −106.11 and −102,47 kJ/mol, respectively.Several studies have shownthat H atoms of inhibitors interact classically with transition metal centers [110].

An additional method of determining how inhibitors interact with the surface can beper formed using charge density differences (CDDs). Based on DFT calculations, this tool shows electron distribution due to chemical/physical bonds by comparing the system of interest with its reference. In this study, CDDs for the investigated inhibitors on the Fe surface are shown in Fig. 14. The Fe–O and Fe–N chemical bonds are noticeably illustrated by significant charge redistribution between O/N and Fe atoms. The greater charge localization of non-classical bonds between Fe and H causes hexamethylenediamine to exhibitstronger adsorption.

In contrast to H+, abundant in the solution, the quantity of solubilized oxygen and carbon dioxide available may need to be considered since the tested solutions were not degassed throughout the tests. Thus, H+ is the primary cause of corrosion. In a 1 M HCl solution, carbon steel undergoes an anodic process known as Fe dissolving and a cathodic reaction known as hydrogen evolution. The inhibition mechanism of N-ECDs and N-HCDs in acidic environments is thought to build a protective coating by adsorbing the inhibitor molecules onto the metal surface to delay corrosion, as shown in Fig. 15
[111]. This process occurs when the N-ECDs and N-HCDs are introduced. Based on the investigation, the effective suppression was facilitated by the coupled chemisorption and physical adsorption of these N-ECDs and N-HCDs. First, through electrostatic contact, the N-ECDs and N-HCDs molecules may adsorb onto the steel surface (physical adsorption). Then, N-ECDs, N-HCDs, and the steel surface share electrons to cause chemisorption. The chemical makeup and charge distribution of the N-ECDs and N-HCDs are crucial to adsorption. The N-ECDs and N-HCDs comprise N atoms, and the chemical adsorption is carried out by O atoms with lone electron pairs. These atoms may interact with the empty Fe atom's 3d orbital to establish a coordination bonding. In the meantime, N-ECDs and N-HCDs' unoccupied π* (anti-bonding) orbitals may receive an electron from the unfilled 3d of the Fe atom, enhancing adsorption on the steel surface [112]. Even if it is unable to make a coordination bond with the steel surface, the rest of the N atoms in the N-ECDs and N-HCDs may, nonetheless, create a barrier via electrostatic contact. In order to block acid assault effectively, the O atom with a single electron pair may also make it easier for N-ECDs and N-HCDs to adsorb onto the steel surface. Furthermore, the carbon-rich hydrophobic structure of N-ECDs and N-HCDs might enhance inhibitory efficacy by erecting a barrier against the intrusion of hostile ions in bulk solution [113].

The nature of the steel surface, the makeup of the hostile surroundings, and other factors influence the inhibition efficiency in addition to the chemical and electrical structures of the N-ECDs and N-HCDs. The carbon steel surface in a 1 M HCl solution has a net positive charge, which will first adsorb the anion, such as Cl-, in the solution. This will produce a surplus negative charge and encourage the adsorption of cations. Because of the electrostatic interactions, the protonation of N may be adsorbed onto the steel surface after the anion (Cl-) adsorption in the presence of N-ECDs and N-HCDs [114].
As a result, the N-ECDs and N-HCDs produced outstanding surface coverage and, consequently, high inhibition for the effective physical adsorption and chemisorption of carbon steel in HCl solution.

Acidification is a technique employed by the petroleum and gas sector to get rid of corrosion deposits within metal pipelines. A more acidic solution (HCl and H2SO4) was utilized for acidification throughout these procedures. Metal materials' surfaces were severely corroded as a consequence. To prevent the metal substance from being destroyed by corrosion, anti-corrosion agents were incorporated into the acidification environment. There are several corrosion inhibitors in use today. Of these, carbon dots corrosion inhibitors are the most effective and safe for the environment. As seen in Fig. 16 , we contrasted the corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-ECDs and N-HCDs with recently published corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in HCl solutions. The effectiveness of some carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors, such as 5-hydroxyisopthalic acid carbon dots (MH-CDs) [115], p-phenylenediamine carbon dots (p-PDCDs) [116], citric acid-based carbon dots (IM-CDs) [117], N, Sco-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) [118], methacrylic acid-based carbon dots (Ma-CDs) [119], ethylenediaminecarbon dots (EDA-CDs) [120], amphiphilic carbon dots (C12-CDs) [121], tryptophan carbon dots (Tr-CDs) [122], Schiff base structurescarbon dots (Sc-CDs) [123]andcitricacid, urea and ionized carbon dots(UI-CDs) [124], were assembled. Comparing N-ECDs and N-HCDs with these documented corrosion inhibitors, it seems that a smaller amount of N-ECDs and N-HCDs may produce a better corrosion inhibition effectiveness, indicating N-ECDs and N-HCDs 's superior anticorrosion potential.

Environmentally friendly N-ECDs and N-HCDs were produced by simple hydrothermal synthesis and further characterized using a variety of methods. After carefully examining their capacity to reduce the corrosion of carbon steel in a solution of 1 M HCl, we found that at 293 K and 80 ppm, N-ECDs and N-HCDs had inhibitory efficacy of up to 94.5 % and 93.6 %, respectively. A Langmuir adsorption isotherm was followed by the adsorption of N-ECDs and N-HCDs on the surface of carbon steel. The absorption of N-ECDs and N-HCDs was linked to both chemisorption and physisorption.. The surface morphology observation indicates that the N-ECDs and N-HCDs create an adsorption protective layer on the steel surface, which acts as a barrier against the corrosive assault of aggressive ions to the steel surface in solution. The formation of the N-ECDs and N-HCDs adsorption layer on the carbon steel surface is confirmed by scanning electron microscopy analysis of the surface morphology. It provides strong support for the electrochemical results. These electrochemical test results are corroborated by quantum chemical computations.
